This high level of integration minimizes the need for separate, discrete components, thereby enhancing [power efficiency](/wiki/Power_efficiency "Power efficiency") and simplifying device design.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Micro-electronic component

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Samsung-Exynos-4412-Quad_SoC_used_in_I9300.jpg/250px-Samsung-Exynos-4412-Quad_SoC_used_in_I9300.jpg)](/wiki/File:Samsung-Exynos-4412-Quad_SoC_used_in_I9300.jpg)

An Exynos 4 Quad (4412), on the circuit board of a [Samsung Galaxy S III](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_S_III "Samsung Galaxy S III") smartphone

A **system on a chip**, or **system on chip** (**SoC**), is an [integrated circuit](/wiki/Integrated_circuit "Integrated circuit") that combines most or all key components of a computer or electronic system onto a single [microchip](/wiki/Microchip "Microchip").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Typically, an SoC includes a [central processing unit](/wiki/Central_processing_unit "Central processing unit") (CPU) with [memory](/wiki/Computer_memory "Computer memory"), [input/output](/wiki/Input/output "Input/output"), and [data storage](/wiki/Computer_data_storage#Secondary_storage "Computer data storage") control functions, along with optional features like a [graphics processing unit](/wiki/Graphics_processing_unit "Graphics processing unit") (GPU), [Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi") connectivity, and radio frequency processing. This high level of integration minimizes the need for separate, discrete components, thereby enhancing [power efficiency](/wiki/Power_efficiency "Power efficiency") and simplifying device design.

High-performance SoCs are often paired with dedicated memory, such as [LPDDR](/wiki/LPDDR "LPDDR"), and flash storage chips, such as [eUFS](/wiki/EUFS "EUFS") or [eMMC](/wiki/EMMC "EMMC"), which may be stacked directly on top of the SoC in a [package-on-package](/wiki/Package-on-package "Package-on-package") (PoP) configuration or placed nearby on the motherboard. Some SoCs also operate alongside specialized chips, such as [cellular modems](/wiki/Mobile_broadband_modem "Mobile broadband modem").[[2]](#cite_note-QS_1-2)

Fundamentally, SoCs integrate one or more [processor cores](/wiki/Processor_core "Processor core") with critical peripherals. This comprehensive integration is conceptually similar to how a [microcontroller](/wiki/Microcontroller "Microcontroller") is designed, but providing far greater computational power. This unified design delivers lower power consumption and a reduced [semiconductor die](/wiki/Semiconductor_die "Semiconductor die") area compared to traditional multi-chip architectures, though at the cost of reduced modularity and component replaceability.

SoCs are ubiquitous in mobile computing, where compact, energy-efficient designs are critical. They power [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone"), [tablets](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer"), and [smartwatches](/wiki/Smartwatch "Smartwatch"), and are increasingly important in [edge computing](/wiki/Edge_computing "Edge computing"), where real-time data processing occurs close to the data source.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Types")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/ARMSoCBlockDiagram.svg/330px-ARMSoCBlockDiagram.svg.png)](/wiki/File:ARMSoCBlockDiagram.svg)

[Block diagram](/wiki/Block_diagram "Block diagram") of a SoC built around a [microcontroller](/wiki/Microcontroller "Microcontroller")

In general, there are three distinguishable types of SoCs:

* SoCs built around a [microcontroller](/wiki/Microcontroller "Microcontroller"),
* SoCs built around a [microprocessor](/wiki/Microprocessor "Microprocessor"), often found in mobile phones;
* Specialized [application-specific integrated circuit](/wiki/Application-specific_integrated_circuit "Application-specific integrated circuit") SoCs designed for specific applications that do not fit into the above two categories.

## Applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Applications")]

SoCs can be applied to any computing task. However, they are typically used in mobile computing such as tablets, smartphones, smartwatches, and netbooks as well as [embedded systems](/wiki/Embedded_system "Embedded system") and in applications where previously [microcontrollers](/wiki/Microcontroller "Microcontroller") would be used.

### Embedded systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Embedded systems")]

Where previously only microcontrollers could be used, SoCs are rising to prominence in the embedded systems market. Tighter system integration offers better reliability and [mean time between failure](/wiki/Mean_time_between_failure "Mean time between failure"), and SoCs offer more advanced functionality and computing power than microcontrollers.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Applications include [AI acceleration](/wiki/AI_accelerator "AI accelerator"), embedded [machine vision](/wiki/Machine_vision "Machine vision"),[[6]](#cite_note-6) [data collection](/wiki/Data_collection "Data collection"), [telemetry](/wiki/Telemetry "Telemetry"), [vector processing](/wiki/Vector_processing "Vector processing") and [ambient intelligence](/wiki/Ambient_intelligence "Ambient intelligence"). Often embedded SoCs target the [internet of things](/wiki/Internet_of_things "Internet of things"), multimedia, networking, telecommunications and [edge computing](/wiki/Edge_computing "Edge computing") markets. Some examples of SoCs for embedded applications include the [STMicroelectronics](/wiki/STMicroelectronics "STMicroelectronics") [STM32](/wiki/STM32 "STM32"), the [Raspberry Pi Ltd](/wiki/Raspberry_Pi_Ltd "Raspberry Pi Ltd") [RP2040](/wiki/RP2040 "RP2040"), and the [AMD](/wiki/AMD "AMD") [Zynq 7000](/wiki/Zynq_7000 "Zynq 7000").

### Mobile computing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Mobile computing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Nokia-9110-4.jpg/250px-Nokia-9110-4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nokia-9110-4.jpg)

System on a chip [AMD Élan](/wiki/AMD_%C3%89lan "AMD Élan") SC450 in [Nokia 9110 Communicator](/wiki/Nokia_9110_Communicator "Nokia 9110 Communicator")

[Mobile computing](/wiki/Mobile_computing "Mobile computing") based SoCs always bundle processors, memories, on-chip [caches](/wiki/Cache_(computing) "Cache (computing)"), [wireless networking](/wiki/Wireless_networking "Wireless networking") capabilities and often [digital camera](/wiki/Digital_camera "Digital camera") hardware and firmware. With increasing memory sizes, high end SoCs will often have no memory and flash storage and instead, the memory and [flash memory](/wiki/Flash_memory "Flash memory") will be placed right next to, or above ([package on package](/wiki/Package_on_package "Package on package")), the SoC.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Some examples of mobile computing SoCs include:

* [Samsung Electronics](/wiki/Samsung_Electronics "Samsung Electronics"): [list](/wiki/List_of_Samsung_System_on_Chips "List of Samsung System on Chips"), typically based on [ARM](/wiki/ARM_architecture_family "ARM architecture family")
  + [Exynos](/wiki/Exynos "Exynos"), used mainly by Samsung's [Galaxy](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy "Samsung Galaxy") series of smartphones
* [Qualcomm](/wiki/Qualcomm "Qualcomm"):
  + [Snapdragon](/wiki/Qualcomm_Snapdragon "Qualcomm Snapdragon") ([list](/wiki/List_of_Qualcomm_Snapdragon_systems-on-chip "List of Qualcomm Snapdragon systems-on-chip")), used in many smartphones. In 2018, Snapdragon SoCs were being used as the backbone of [laptop computers](/wiki/Laptop_computer "Laptop computer") running [Windows 10](/wiki/Windows_10 "Windows 10"), marketed as "Always Connected PCs".[[8]](#cite_note-:3-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:4-9)
* [MediaTek](/wiki/MediaTek "MediaTek"), typically based on ARM
  + [Dimensity](/wiki/List_of_MediaTek_systems_on_chips "List of MediaTek systems on chips") & Kompanio Series. Standalone application & tablet processors that power devices such as Amazon Echo Show

### Personal computers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Personal computers")]

In 1992, [Acorn Computers](/wiki/Acorn_Computers "Acorn Computers") produced the [A3010, A3020 and A4000 range of personal computers](/wiki/Acorn_Archimedes#New_range_and_a_laptop "Acorn Archimedes") with the ARM250 SoC. It combined the original Acorn ARM2 processor with a memory controller (MEMC), video controller (VIDC), and I/O controller (IOC). In previous Acorn [ARM](/wiki/ARM_architecture "ARM architecture")-powered computers, these were four discrete chips. The ARM7500 chip was their second-generation SoC, based on the ARM700, VIDC20 and IOMD controllers, and was widely licensed in embedded devices such as set-top-boxes, as well as later Acorn personal computers.

Tablet and laptop manufacturers have learned lessons from embedded systems and smartphone markets about reduced power consumption, better performance and reliability from tighter [integration](/wiki/System_integration "System integration") of hardware and [firmware](/wiki/Firmware "Firmware") [modules](/wiki/Module_system "Module system"), and [LTE](/wiki/LTE_(telecommunication) "LTE (telecommunication)") and other [wireless network](/wiki/Wireless_network "Wireless network") communications integrated on chip (integrated [network interface controllers](/wiki/Network_interface_controller "Network interface controller")).[[10]](#cite_note-10)

On modern laptops and mini PCs, the low-power variants of [AMD Ryzen](/wiki/AMD_Ryzen "AMD Ryzen") and [Intel Core](/wiki/Intel_Core "Intel Core") processors use SoC design integrating CPU, IGPU, chipset and other processors in a single package. However, such x86 processors still require external memory and storage chips.

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Structure")]

An SoC consists of hardware [functional units](/wiki/Functional_unit "Functional unit"), including [microprocessors](/wiki/Microprocessor "Microprocessor") that run [software code](/wiki/Software_code "Software code"), as well as a [communications subsystem](/wiki/Communications_subsystem "Communications subsystem") to connect, control, direct and interface between these functional modules.

### Functional components

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Functional components")]

#### Processor cores

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Processor cores")]

An SoC must have at least one [processor core](/wiki/Processor_core "Processor core"), but typically an SoC has more than one core. Processor cores can be a [microcontroller](/wiki/Microcontroller "Microcontroller"), [microprocessor](/wiki/Microprocessor "Microprocessor") (μP),[[11]](#cite_note-Furber_ARM-11) [digital signal processor](/wiki/Digital_signal_processor "Digital signal processor") (DSP) or [application-specific instruction set processor](/wiki/Application-specific_instruction_set_processor "Application-specific instruction set processor") (ASIP) core.[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12) ASIPs have [instruction sets](/wiki/Instruction_set_architecture "Instruction set architecture") that are customized for an [application domain](/wiki/Application_domain "Application domain") and designed to be more efficient than general-purpose instructions for a specific type of workload. Multiprocessor SoCs have more than one processor core by definition. The [ARM architecture](/wiki/ARM_architecture "ARM architecture") is a common choice for SoC processor cores because some ARM-architecture cores are [soft processors](/wiki/Soft_processor "Soft processor") specified as [IP cores](/wiki/IP_core "IP core").[[11]](#cite_note-Furber_ARM-11)

#### Memory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Memory")]

Further information: [Computer memory](/wiki/Computer_memory "Computer memory")

SoCs must have [semiconductor memory](/wiki/Semiconductor_memory "Semiconductor memory") blocks to perform their computation, as do [microcontrollers](/wiki/Microcontroller "Microcontroller") and other [embedded systems](/wiki/Embedded_system "Embedded system"). Depending on the application, SoC memory may form a [memory hierarchy](/wiki/Memory_hierarchy "Memory hierarchy") and [cache hierarchy](/wiki/Cache_hierarchy "Cache hierarchy"). In the mobile computing market, this is common, but in many [low-power](/wiki/Low-power_electronics "Low-power electronics") embedded microcontrollers, this is not necessary. Memory technologies for SoCs include [read-only memory](/wiki/Read-only_memory "Read-only memory") (ROM), [random-access memory](/wiki/Random-access_memory "Random-access memory") (RAM), Electrically Erasable Programmable ROM ([EEPROM](/wiki/EEPROM "EEPROM")) and [flash memory](/wiki/Flash_memory "Flash memory").[[11]](#cite_note-Furber_ARM-11) As in other computer systems, RAM can be subdivided into relatively faster but more expensive [static RAM](/wiki/Static_RAM "Static RAM") (SRAM) and the slower but cheaper [dynamic RAM](/wiki/Dynamic_RAM "Dynamic RAM") (DRAM). When an SoC has a [cache](/wiki/Cache_(computing) "Cache (computing)") hierarchy, SRAM will usually be used to implement [processor registers](/wiki/Processor_register "Processor register") and cores' [built-in caches](/wiki/CPU_cache "CPU cache") whereas DRAM will be used for [main memory](/wiki/Main_memory "Main memory"). "Main memory" may be specific to a single processor (which can be [multi-core](/wiki/Multi-core "Multi-core")) when the SoC [has multiple processors](/wiki/Multi-processor_system-on-chip "Multi-processor system-on-chip"), in this case it is [distributed memory](/wiki/Distributed_memory "Distributed memory") and must be sent via [§ Intermodule communication](#Intermodule_communication) on-chip to be accessed by a different processor.[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12) For further discussion of multi-processing memory issues, see [cache coherence](/wiki/Cache_coherence "Cache coherence") and [memory latency](/wiki/Memory_latency "Memory latency").

#### Interfaces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Interfaces")]

SoCs include external [interfaces](/wiki/Electrical_connector "Electrical connector"), typically for [communication protocols](/wiki/Communication_protocol "Communication protocol"). These are often based upon industry standards such as [USB](/wiki/USB "USB"), [Ethernet](/wiki/Ethernet "Ethernet"), [USART](/wiki/USART "USART"), [SPI](/wiki/Serial_Peripheral_Interface "Serial Peripheral Interface"), [HDMI](/wiki/HDMI "HDMI"), [I²C](/wiki/I%C2%B2C "I²C"), [CSI](/wiki/Camera_Serial_Interface "Camera Serial Interface"), etc. These interfaces will differ according to the intended application. [Wireless networking](/wiki/Wireless_network "Wireless network") protocols such as [Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi"), [Bluetooth](/wiki/Bluetooth "Bluetooth"), [6LoWPAN](/wiki/6LoWPAN "6LoWPAN") and [near-field communication](/wiki/Near-field_communication "Near-field communication") may also be supported.

When needed, SoCs include [analog](/wiki/Analog_signal "Analog signal") interfaces including [analog-to-digital](/wiki/Analog-to-digital "Analog-to-digital") and [digital-to-analog converters](/wiki/Digital-to-analog_converter "Digital-to-analog converter"), often for [signal processing](/wiki/Signal_processing "Signal processing"). These may be able to interface with different types of [sensors](/wiki/Sensor "Sensor") or [actuators](/wiki/Actuator "Actuator"), including [smart transducers](/wiki/Smart_transducer "Smart transducer"). They may interface with application-specific [modules](/wiki/Modularity "Modularity") or shields.[[nb 1]](#cite_note-13) Or they may be internal to the SoC, such as if an analog sensor is built in to the SoC and its readings must be converted to digital signals for mathematical processing.

#### Digital signal processors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Digital signal processors")]

[Digital signal processor](/wiki/Digital_signal_processor "Digital signal processor") (DSP) cores are often included on SoCs. They perform [signal processing](/wiki/Signal_processing "Signal processing") operations in SoCs for [sensors](/wiki/Sensor "Sensor"), [actuators](/wiki/Actuator "Actuator"), [data collection](/wiki/Data_collection "Data collection"), [data analysis](/wiki/Data_analysis "Data analysis") and multimedia processing. DSP cores typically feature [very long instruction word](/wiki/Very_long_instruction_word "Very long instruction word") (VLIW) and [single instruction, multiple data](/wiki/Single_instruction,_multiple_data "Single instruction, multiple data") (SIMD) [instruction set architectures](/wiki/Instruction_set_architecture "Instruction set architecture"), and are therefore highly amenable to exploiting [instruction-level parallelism](/wiki/Instruction-level_parallelism "Instruction-level parallelism") through [parallel processing](/wiki/Parallel_processing_(DSP_implementation) "Parallel processing (DSP implementation)") and [superscalar execution](/wiki/Superscalar_execution "Superscalar execution").[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12): 4 SP cores most often feature application-specific instructions, and as such are typically [application-specific instruction set processors](/wiki/Application-specific_instruction_set_processor "Application-specific instruction set processor") (ASIP). Such application-specific instructions correspond to dedicated hardware [functional units](/wiki/Functional_unit "Functional unit") that compute those instructions.

Typical DSP instructions include [multiply-accumulate](/wiki/Multiply-accumulate "Multiply-accumulate"), [Fast Fourier transform](/wiki/Fast_Fourier_transform "Fast Fourier transform"), [fused multiply-add](/wiki/Fused_multiply-accumulate "Fused multiply-accumulate"), and [convolutions](/wiki/Convolution "Convolution").

#### Other

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Other")]

As with other computer systems, SoCs require [timing sources](/wiki/Clock_generator "Clock generator") to generate [clock signals](/wiki/Clock_signal "Clock signal"), control execution of SoC functions and provide time context to [signal processing](/wiki/Signal_processing "Signal processing") applications of the SoC, if needed. Popular time sources are [crystal oscillators](/wiki/Crystal_oscillators "Crystal oscillators") and [phase-locked loops](/wiki/Phase-locked_loop "Phase-locked loop").

SoC [peripherals](/wiki/Peripheral "Peripheral") including [counter](/wiki/Counter_(digital) "Counter (digital)")-timers, real-time [timers](/wiki/Timer "Timer") and [power-on reset](/wiki/Power-on_reset "Power-on reset") generators. SoCs also include [voltage regulators](/wiki/Voltage_regulator "Voltage regulator") and [power management](/wiki/Power_management "Power management") circuits.

### Intermodule communication

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Intermodule communication")]

SoCs comprise many [execution units](/wiki/Execution_unit "Execution unit"). These units must often send data and [instructions](/wiki/Instruction_(computing) "Instruction (computing)") back and forth. Because of this, all but the most trivial SoCs require [communications subsystems](/wiki/Communications_system "Communications system"). Originally, as with other [microcomputer](/wiki/Microcomputer "Microcomputer") technologies, [data bus](/wiki/Data_bus "Data bus") architectures were used, but recently designs based on sparse intercommunication networks known as [networks-on-chip](/wiki/Network_on_a_chip "Network on a chip") (NoC) have risen to prominence and are forecast to overtake bus architectures for SoC design in the near future.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-14)

#### Bus-based communication

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Bus-based communication")]

Historically, a shared global [computer bus](/wiki/Computer_bus "Computer bus") typically connected the different components, also called "blocks" of the SoC.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-14) A very common bus for SoC communications is ARM's royalty-free Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture ([AMBA](/wiki/Advanced_Microcontroller_Bus_Architecture "Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture")) standard.

[Direct memory access](/wiki/Direct_memory_access "Direct memory access") controllers route data directly between external interfaces and SoC memory, bypassing the CPU or [control unit](/wiki/Control_unit "Control unit"), thereby increasing the data [throughput](/wiki/Throughput "Throughput") of the SoC. This is similar to some [device drivers](/wiki/Device_driver "Device driver") of peripherals on component-based [multi-chip module](/wiki/Multi-chip_module "Multi-chip module") PC architectures.

Wire delay is not scalable due to continued [miniaturization](/wiki/Miniaturization "Miniaturization"), [system performance](/wiki/Computer_performance "Computer performance") does not scale with the number of cores attached, the SoC's [operating frequency](/wiki/Operating_frequency "Operating frequency") must decrease with each additional core attached for power to be sustainable, and long wires consume large amounts of electrical power. These challenges are prohibitive to supporting [manycore](/wiki/Manycore_processor "Manycore processor") systems on chip.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-14): xiii

#### Network on a chip

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Network on a chip")]

Main article: [Network on a chip](/wiki/Network_on_a_chip "Network on a chip")

In the late 2010s, a trend of SoCs implementing [communications subsystems](/wiki/Communications_subsystem "Communications subsystem") in terms of a network-like topology instead of [bus-based](/wiki/Bus_(computing) "Bus (computing)") protocols has emerged. A trend towards more processor cores on SoCs has caused on-chip communication efficiency to become one of the key factors in determining the overall system performance and cost.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-14): xiii This has led to the emergence of interconnection networks with [router](/wiki/Router_(computing) "Router (computing)")-based [packet switching](/wiki/Packet_switching "Packet switching") known as "[networks on chip](/wiki/Network_on_a_chip "Network on a chip")" (NoCs) to overcome the [bottlenecks](/wiki/Bottleneck_(engineering) "Bottleneck (engineering)") of bus-based networks.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-14): xiii

Networks-on-chip have advantages including destination- and application-specific [routing](/wiki/Routing "Routing"), greater power efficiency and reduced possibility of [bus contention](/wiki/Bus_contention "Bus contention"). Network-on-chip architectures take inspiration from [communication protocols](/wiki/Communication_protocols "Communication protocols") like [TCP](/wiki/Transmission_Control_Protocol "Transmission Control Protocol") and the [Internet protocol suite](/wiki/Internet_protocol_suite "Internet protocol suite") for on-chip communication,[[13]](#cite_note-:0-14) although they typically have fewer [network layers](/wiki/Network_layer "Network layer"). Optimal network-on-chip [network architectures](/wiki/Network_architecture "Network architecture") are an ongoing area of much research interest. NoC architectures range from traditional distributed computing [network topologies](/wiki/Network_topologies "Network topologies") such as [torus](/wiki/Torus_interconnect "Torus interconnect"), [hypercube](/wiki/Hypercube_internetwork_topology "Hypercube internetwork topology"), [meshes](/wiki/Mesh_networking "Mesh networking") and [tree networks](/wiki/Tree_network "Tree network") to [genetic algorithm scheduling](/wiki/Genetic_algorithm_scheduling "Genetic algorithm scheduling") to [randomized algorithms](/wiki/Randomized_algorithm "Randomized algorithm") such as [random walks with branching](/wiki/Branching_random_walk "Branching random walk") and randomized [time to live](/wiki/Time_to_live "Time to live") (TTL).

Many SoC researchers consider NoC architectures to be the future of SoC design because they have been shown to efficiently meet power and throughput needs of SoC designs. Current NoC architectures are two-dimensional. 2D IC design has limited [floorplanning](/wiki/Floorplanning "Floorplanning") choices as the number of cores in SoCs increase, so as [three-dimensional integrated circuits](/wiki/Three-dimensional_integrated_circuit "Three-dimensional integrated circuit") (3DICs) emerge, SoC designers are looking towards building three-dimensional on-chip networks known as 3DNoCs.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-14)

## Design flow

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Design flow")]

Main articles: [Electronics design flow](/wiki/Design_flow_(EDA) "Design flow (EDA)"), [Physical design (electronics)](/wiki/Physical_design_(electronics) "Physical design (electronics)"), and [Platform-based design](/wiki/Platform-based_design "Platform-based design")

See also: [Systems design](/wiki/Systems_design "Systems design") and [Software design process](/wiki/Software_design "Software design")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/SoCDesignFlow.svg/330px-SoCDesignFlow.svg.png)](/wiki/File:SoCDesignFlow.svg)

SoC design flow

A system on a chip consists of both the [hardware](/wiki/Electronic_hardware "Electronic hardware"), described in [§ Structure](#Structure), and the software controlling the microcontroller, microprocessor or digital signal processor cores, peripherals and interfaces. The [design flow](/wiki/Design_flow "Design flow") for an SoC aims to develop this hardware and software at the same time, also known as architectural co-design. The design flow must also take into account optimizations ([§ Optimization goals](#Optimization_goals)) and constraints.

Most SoCs are developed from pre-qualified hardware component [IP core specifications](/wiki/Semiconductor_intellectual_property_core "Semiconductor intellectual property core") for the hardware elements and [execution units](/wiki/Execution_unit "Execution unit"), collectively "blocks", described above, together with software [device drivers](/wiki/Device_driver "Device driver") that may control their operation. Of particular importance are the [protocol stacks](/wiki/Protocol_stack "Protocol stack") that drive industry-standard interfaces like [USB](/wiki/USB "USB"). The hardware blocks are put together using [computer-aided design](/wiki/Computer-aided_design "Computer-aided design") tools, specifically [electronic design automation](/wiki/Electronic_design_automation "Electronic design automation") tools; the [software modules](/wiki/Modular_programming "Modular programming") are integrated using a software [integrated development environment](/wiki/Integrated_development_environment "Integrated development environment").

SoCs components are also often designed in [high-level programming languages](/wiki/High-level_programming_language "High-level programming language") such as [C++](/wiki/C%2B%2B "C++"), [MATLAB](/wiki/MATLAB "MATLAB") or [SystemC](/wiki/SystemC "SystemC") and converted to [RTL](/wiki/Register-transfer_level "Register-transfer level") designs through [high-level synthesis](/wiki/High-level_synthesis "High-level synthesis") (HLS) tools such as [C to HDL](/wiki/C_to_HDL "C to HDL") or [flow to HDL](/wiki/Flow_to_HDL "Flow to HDL").[[14]](#cite_note-15) HLS products called "algorithmic synthesis" allow designers to use C++ to model and synthesize system, circuit, software and verification levels all in one high level language commonly known to [computer engineers](/wiki/Computer_engineers "Computer engineers") in a manner independent of time scales, which are typically specified in HDL.[[15]](#cite_note-16) Other components can remain software and be compiled and embedded onto [soft-core processors](/wiki/Soft_microprocessor "Soft microprocessor") included in the SoC as modules in HDL as [IP cores](/wiki/Semiconductor_intellectual_property_core "Semiconductor intellectual property core").

Once the [architecture](/wiki/Computer_architecture "Computer architecture") of the SoC has been defined, any new hardware elements are written in an abstract [hardware description language](/wiki/Hardware_description_language "Hardware description language") termed [register transfer level](/wiki/Register_transfer_level "Register transfer level") (RTL) which defines the circuit behavior, or synthesized into RTL from a high level language through high-level synthesis. These elements are connected together in a hardware description language to create the full SoC design. The logic specified to connect these components and convert between possibly different interfaces provided by different vendors is called [glue logic](/wiki/Glue_logic "Glue logic").

### Design verification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Design verification")]

Further information: [Functional verification](/wiki/Functional_verification "Functional verification") and [Signoff (electronic design automation)](/wiki/Signoff_(electronic_design_automation) "Signoff (electronic design automation)")

Chips are verified for validation correctness before being sent to a [semiconductor foundry](/wiki/Semiconductor_foundry "Semiconductor foundry"). This process is called [functional verification](/wiki/Functional_verification "Functional verification") and it accounts for a significant portion of the time and energy expended in the [chip design life cycle](/wiki/Integrated_circuit_development "Integrated circuit development"), often quoted as 70%.[[16]](#cite_note-70%_verification?-17)[[17]](#cite_note-verification_vs._validation-18) With the growing complexity of chips, [hardware verification languages](/wiki/Hardware_verification_language "Hardware verification language") like [SystemVerilog](/wiki/SystemVerilog "SystemVerilog"), [SystemC](/wiki/SystemC "SystemC"), [e](/wiki/E_(verification_language) "E (verification language)"), and OpenVera are being used. [Bugs](/wiki/Software_bug "Software bug") found in the verification stage are reported to the designer.

Traditionally, engineers have employed simulation acceleration, [emulation](/wiki/Emulator "Emulator") or prototyping on [reprogrammable hardware](/wiki/Reconfigurable_computing "Reconfigurable computing") to verify and debug hardware and software for SoC designs prior to the finalization of the design, known as [tape-out](/wiki/Tape-out "Tape-out"). [Field-programmable gate arrays](/wiki/Field-programmable_gate_array "Field-programmable gate array") (FPGAs) are favored for prototyping SoCs because [FPGA prototypes](/wiki/FPGA_prototyping "FPGA prototyping") are reprogrammable, allow [debugging](/wiki/Debugging "Debugging") and are more flexible than [application-specific integrated circuits](/wiki/Application-specific_integrated_circuit "Application-specific integrated circuit") (ASICs).[[18]](#cite_note-nm_prototyping-19)[[19]](#cite_note-Reason_to_debug_in_FPGA-20)

With high capacity and fast compilation time, simulation acceleration and emulation are powerful technologies that provide wide visibility into systems. Both technologies, however, operate slowly, on the order of MHz, which may be significantly slower – up to 100 times slower – than the SoC's operating frequency. Acceleration and emulation boxes are also very large and expensive at over US$1 million.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

FPGA prototypes, in contrast, use FPGAs directly to enable engineers to validate and test at, or close to, a system's full operating frequency with real-world stimuli. Tools such as Certus[[20]](#cite_note-21) are used to insert probes in the FPGA RTL that make signals available for observation. This is used to debug hardware, firmware and software interactions across multiple FPGAs with capabilities similar to a logic analyzer.

In parallel, the hardware elements are grouped and passed through a process of [logic synthesis](/wiki/Logic_synthesis "Logic synthesis"), during which performance constraints, such as operational frequency and expected signal delays, are applied. This generates an output known as a [netlist](/wiki/Netlist "Netlist") describing the design as a physical circuit and its interconnections. These netlists are combined with the [glue logic](/wiki/Glue_logic "Glue logic") connecting the components to produce the schematic description of the SoC as a circuit which can be [printed](/wiki/Printed_circuit_board "Printed circuit board") onto a chip. This process is known as [place and route](/wiki/Place_and_route "Place and route") and precedes [tape-out](/wiki/Tape-out "Tape-out") in the event that the SoCs are produced as [application-specific integrated circuits](/wiki/Application-specific_integrated_circuit "Application-specific integrated circuit") (ASIC).

## Optimization goals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Optimization goals")]

SoCs must optimize [power use](/wiki/Power_consumption "Power consumption"), area on [die](/wiki/Die_(integrated_circuit) "Die (integrated circuit)"), communication, positioning for [locality](/wiki/Locality_of_reference "Locality of reference") between modular units and other factors. Optimization is necessarily a design goal of SoCs. If optimization was not necessary, the engineers would use a [multi-chip module](/wiki/Multi-chip_module "Multi-chip module") architecture without accounting for the area use, power consumption or performance of the system to the same extent.

Common optimization targets for SoC designs follow, with explanations of each. In general, optimizing any of these quantities may be a hard [combinatorial optimization](/wiki/Combinatorial_optimization "Combinatorial optimization") problem, and can indeed be [NP-hard](/wiki/NP-hard "NP-hard") fairly easily. Therefore, sophisticated [optimization algorithms](/wiki/Optimization_algorithm "Optimization algorithm") are often required and it may be practical to use [approximation algorithms](/wiki/Approximation_algorithm "Approximation algorithm") or [heuristics](/wiki/Heuristic_(computer_science) "Heuristic (computer science)") in some cases. Additionally, most SoC designs contain [multiple variables to optimize simultaneously](/wiki/Multivariate_optimization "Multivariate optimization"), so [Pareto efficient](/wiki/Pareto_efficient "Pareto efficient") solutions are sought after in SoC design. Oftentimes the goals of optimizing some of these quantities are directly at odds, further adding complexity to design optimization of SoCs and introducing [trade-offs](/wiki/Trade-off#Engineering "Trade-off") in system design.

For broader coverage of trade-offs and [requirements analysis](/wiki/Requirements_analysis "Requirements analysis"), see [requirements engineering](/wiki/Requirements_engineering "Requirements engineering").

### Targets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Targets")]

#### Power consumption

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Power consumption")]

SoCs are optimized to minimize the [electrical power](/wiki/Electric_power#Definition "Electric power") used to perform the SoC's functions. Most SoCs must use low power. SoC systems often require long [battery life](/wiki/Battery_life "Battery life") (such as [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone")), can potentially spend months or years without a power source while needing to maintain autonomous function, and often are limited in power use by a high number of [embedded](/wiki/Embedded_system "Embedded system") SoCs being [networked together](/wiki/Distributed_computing "Distributed computing") in an area. Additionally, energy costs can be high and conserving energy will reduce the [total cost of ownership](/wiki/Total_cost_of_ownership "Total cost of ownership") of the SoC. Finally, [waste heat](/wiki/Waste_heat "Waste heat") from high energy consumption can damage other circuit components if too much heat is dissipated, giving another pragmatic reason to conserve energy. The amount of energy used in a circuit is the [integral](/wiki/Integral "Integral") of [power](/wiki/Power_(physics) "Power (physics)") consumed with respect to time, and the [average rate](/wiki/Mean_value_theorem "Mean value theorem") of power consumption is the product of [current](/wiki/Electric_current "Electric current") by [voltage](/wiki/Voltage "Voltage"). Equivalently, by [Ohm's law](/wiki/Ohm%27s_law "Ohm's law"), power is current squared times resistance or voltage squared divided by [resistance](/wiki/Resistance_(physics) "Resistance (physics)"):

![{\displaystyle P=IV={\frac {V^{2}}{R}}={I^{2}}{R}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b7ea74b4b52b63f4bf75bcf2eaeecd3c8333bb32)SoCs are frequently embedded in [portable devices](/wiki/Portable_device "Portable device") such as [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphones "Smartphones"), [GPS navigation devices](/wiki/GPS_navigation_device "GPS navigation device"), digital [watches](/wiki/Digital_watch "Digital watch") (including [smartwatches](/wiki/Smartwatch "Smartwatch")) and [netbooks](/wiki/Netbook "Netbook"). Customers want long battery lives for [mobile computing](/wiki/Mobile_computing "Mobile computing") devices, another reason that power consumption must be minimized in SoCs. [Multimedia applications](/wiki/Multimedia_application "Multimedia application") are often executed on these devices, including video games, [video streaming](/wiki/Video_streaming "Video streaming"), [image processing](/wiki/Image_processing "Image processing"); all of which have grown in [computational complexity](/wiki/Computational_complexity "Computational complexity") in recent years with user demands and expectations for higher-[quality](/wiki/Video_quality "Video quality") multimedia. Computation is more demanding as expectations move towards [3D video](/wiki/3D_video "3D video") at [high resolution](/wiki/High_resolution "High resolution") with [multiple standards](/wiki/List_of_video_compression_formats "List of video compression formats"), so SoCs performing multimedia tasks must be computationally capable platform while being low power to run off a standard mobile battery.[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12): 3

#### Performance per watt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Performance per watt")]

See also: [Green computing](/wiki/Green_computing "Green computing")

SoCs are optimized to maximize [power efficiency](/wiki/Power_efficiency "Power efficiency") in performance per watt: maximize the performance of the SoC given a budget of power usage. Many applications such as [edge computing](/wiki/Edge_computing "Edge computing"), [distributed processing](/wiki/Distributed_processing "Distributed processing") and [ambient intelligence](/wiki/Ambient_intelligence "Ambient intelligence") require a certain level of [computational performance](/wiki/Computer_performance "Computer performance"), but power is limited in most SoC environments.

#### Waste heat

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Waste heat")]

Main article: [Heat generation in integrated circuits](/wiki/Heat_generation_in_integrated_circuits "Heat generation in integrated circuits")

See also: [Thermal management in electronics](/wiki/Thermal_management_(electronics) "Thermal management (electronics)") and [Thermal design power](/wiki/Thermal_design_power "Thermal design power")

SoC designs are optimized to minimize [waste heat](/wiki/Waste_heat "Waste heat") [output](/wiki/Dissipation "Dissipation") on the chip. As with other [integrated circuits](/wiki/Integrated_circuit "Integrated circuit"), heat generated due to high [power density](/wiki/Power_density "Power density") are the [bottleneck](/wiki/Bottleneck_(engineering) "Bottleneck (engineering)") to further [miniaturization](/wiki/Miniaturization "Miniaturization") of components.[[21]](#cite_note-:2-22): 1 The power densities of high speed integrated circuits, particularly microprocessors and including SoCs, have become highly uneven. Too much waste heat can damage circuits and erode [reliability](/wiki/Reliability_(semiconductor) "Reliability (semiconductor)") of the circuit over time. High temperatures and thermal stress negatively impact reliability, [stress migration](/wiki/Stress_migration "Stress migration"), decreased [mean time between failures](/wiki/Mean_time_between_failures "Mean time between failures"), [electromigration](/wiki/Electromigration "Electromigration"), [wire bonding](/wiki/Wire_bonding "Wire bonding"), [metastability](/wiki/Metastability_(electronics) "Metastability (electronics)") and other performance degradation of the SoC over time.[[21]](#cite_note-:2-22): 2–9

In particular, most SoCs are in a small physical area or volume and therefore the effects of waste heat are compounded because there is little room for it to diffuse out of the system. Because of high [transistor counts](/wiki/Transistor_count "Transistor count") on modern devices, oftentimes a layout of sufficient throughput and high [transistor density](/wiki/Transistors_density "Transistors density") is physically realizable from [fabrication processes](/wiki/Semiconductor_device_fabrication "Semiconductor device fabrication") but would result in unacceptably high amounts of heat in the circuit's volume.[[21]](#cite_note-:2-22): 1

These thermal effects force SoC and other chip designers to apply conservative [design margins](/wiki/Design_margin "Design margin"), creating less performant devices to mitigate the risk of [catastrophic failure](/wiki/Catastrophic_failure "Catastrophic failure"). Due to increased [transistor densities](/wiki/Transistors_density "Transistors density") as length scales get smaller, each [process generation](/wiki/Semiconductor_node "Semiconductor node") produces more heat output than the last. Compounding this problem, SoC architectures are usually heterogeneous, creating spatially inhomogeneous [heat fluxes](/wiki/Heat_flux "Heat flux"), which cannot be effectively mitigated by uniform [passive cooling](/wiki/Passive_cooling "Passive cooling").[[21]](#cite_note-:2-22): 1

#### Throughput

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Throughput")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=). *(October 2018)* |

SoCs are optimized to maximize computational and communications [throughput](/wiki/Throughput "Throughput").

#### Latency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Latency")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=). *(October 2018)* |

SoCs are optimized to minimize [latency](/wiki/Latency_(engineering) "Latency (engineering)") for some or all of their functions. This can be accomplished by [laying out](/wiki/Integrated_circuit_layout "Integrated circuit layout") elements with proper proximity and [locality](/wiki/Locality_of_reference "Locality of reference") to each-other to minimize the interconnection delays and maximize the speed at which data is communicated between modules, [functional units](/wiki/Execution_unit "Execution unit") and memories. In general, optimizing to minimize latency is an [NP-complete](/wiki/NP-complete "NP-complete") problem equivalent to the [Boolean satisfiability problem](/wiki/Boolean_satisfiability_problem "Boolean satisfiability problem").

For [tasks](/wiki/Task_(computing) "Task (computing)") running on processor cores, latency and throughput can be improved with [task scheduling](/wiki/Task_scheduling "Task scheduling"). Some tasks run in application-specific hardware units, however, and even task scheduling may not be sufficient to optimize all software-based tasks to meet timing and throughput constraints.

### Methodologies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Methodologies")]

Further information: [Multi-objective optimization](/wiki/Multi-objective_optimization "Multi-objective optimization"), [Multiple-criteria decision analysis](/wiki/Multiple-criteria_decision_analysis "Multiple-criteria decision analysis"), and [Architecture tradeoff analysis](/wiki/Architecture_tradeoff_analysis_method "Architecture tradeoff analysis method")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=). *(October 2018)* |

Systems on chip are modeled with standard hardware [verification and validation](/wiki/Verification_and_validation "Verification and validation") techniques, but additional techniques are used to model and optimize SoC design alternatives to make the system optimal with respect to [multiple-criteria decision analysis](/wiki/Multiple-criteria_decision_analysis "Multiple-criteria decision analysis") on the above optimization targets.

#### Task scheduling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Task scheduling")]

[Task scheduling](/wiki/Task_scheduling "Task scheduling") is an important activity in any computer system with multiple [processes](/wiki/Process_(computing) "Process (computing)") or [threads](/wiki/Thread_(computing) "Thread (computing)") sharing a single processor core. It is important to reduce [§ Latency](#Latency) and increase [§ Throughput](#Throughput) for [embedded software](/wiki/Embedded_software "Embedded software") running on an SoC's [§ Processor cores](#Processor_cores). Not every important computing activity in a SoC is performed in software running on on-chip processors, but scheduling can drastically improve performance of software-based tasks and other tasks involving [shared resources](/wiki/Shared_resource "Shared resource").

Software running on SoCs often schedules tasks according to [network scheduling](/wiki/Network_scheduling "Network scheduling") and [randomized scheduling](/wiki/Stochastic_scheduling "Stochastic scheduling") algorithms.

#### Pipelining

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Pipelining")]

Main article: [Pipeline (computing)](/wiki/Pipeline_(computing) "Pipeline (computing)")

Hardware and software tasks are often pipelined in [processor design](/wiki/Processor_design "Processor design"). Pipelining is an important principle for [speedup](/wiki/Speedup "Speedup") in [computer architecture](/wiki/Computer_architecture "Computer architecture"). They are frequently used in [CPUs](/wiki/CPU "CPU") (for example, the [classic RISC pipeline](/wiki/Classic_RISC_pipeline "Classic RISC pipeline")) and [GPUs](/wiki/GPU "GPU") ([graphics pipeline](/wiki/Graphics_pipeline "Graphics pipeline")), but are also applied to application-specific tasks such as [digital signal processing](/wiki/Digital_signal_processing "Digital signal processing") and multimedia manipulations in the context of SoCs.[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12)

#### Probabilistic modeling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Probabilistic modeling")]

SoCs are often analyzed though [probabilistic models](/wiki/Probabilistic_model "Probabilistic model"), [queueing networks](/wiki/Queueing_theory#Queueing_networks "Queueing theory"), and [Markov chains](/wiki/Markov_chain "Markov chain"). For instance, [Little's law](/wiki/Little%27s_law "Little's law") allows SoC states and NoC buffers to be modeled as arrival processes and analyzed through [Poisson random variables](/wiki/Poisson_random_variable "Poisson random variable") and [Poisson processes](/wiki/Poisson_process "Poisson process").

#### Markov chains

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Markov chains")]

SoCs are often modeled with [Markov chains](/wiki/Markov_chain "Markov chain"), both [discrete time](/wiki/Markov_chain#Discrete-time_Markov_chain "Markov chain") and [continuous time](/wiki/Markov_chain#Continuous-time_Markov_chain "Markov chain") variants. Markov chain modeling allows [asymptotic analysis](/wiki/Asymptotic_analysis "Asymptotic analysis") of the SoC's [steady state distribution](/wiki/Markov_chain#Steady-state_analysis_and_limiting_distributions "Markov chain") of power, heat, latency and other factors to allow design decisions to be optimized for the common case.

## Fabrication

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=System_on_a_chip&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Fabrication")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/System_on_a_chip "Special:EditPage/System on a chip") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(March 2017)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Further information: [Semiconductor device fabrication](/wiki/Semiconductor_device_fabrication "Semiconductor device fabrication")

SoC chips are typically [fabricated](/wiki/Semiconductor_device_fabrication "Semiconductor device fabrication") using [metal–oxide–semiconductor](/wiki/Metal%E2%80%93oxide%E2%80%93semiconductor "Metal–oxide–semiconductor") (MOS) technology.[[22]](#cite_note-23) The netlists described above are used as the basis for the physical design ([place and route](/wiki/Place_and_route "Place and route")) flow to convert the designers' intent into the design of the SoC. Throughout this conversion process, the design is analyzed with static timing modeling, simulation and other tools to ensure that it meets the specified operational parameters such as frequency, power consumption and dissipation, functional integrity (as described in the register transfer level code) and electrical integrity.

When all known bugs have been rectified and these have been re-verified and all physical design checks are done, the physical design files describing each layer of the chip are sent to the foundry's mask shop where a full set of glass lithographic masks will be etched. These are sent to a wafer fabrication plant to create the SoC dice before packaging and testing.

SoCs can be fabricated by several technologies, including:

* [Full custom](/wiki/Full_custom "Full custom") [ASIC](/wiki/ASIC "ASIC")
* [Standard cell](/wiki/Standard_cell "Standard cell") ASIC
* [Field-programmable gate array](/wiki/Field-programmable_gate_array "Field-programmable gate array") (FPGA)

ASICs consume less power and are faster than FPGAs but cannot be reprogrammed and are expensive to manufacture. FPGA designs are more suitable for lower volume designs, but after enough units of production ASICs reduce the total cost of ownership.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

SoC designs consume less power and have a lower cost and higher reliability than the multi-chip systems that they replace. With fewer packages in the system, assembly costs are reduced as well.

However, like most [very-large-scale integration](/wiki/Very-large-scale_integration "Very-large-scale integration") (VLSI) designs, the total cost[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] is higher for one large chip than for the same functionality distributed over several smaller chips, because of [lower yields](/wiki/Semiconductor_device_fabrication#Device_test "Semiconductor device fabrication")[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] and higher [non-recurring engineering](/wiki/Non-recurring_engineering "Non-recurring engineering") costs.

When it is not feasible to construct an SoC for a particular application, an alternative is a [system in package](/wiki/System_in_package "System in package") (SiP) comprising a number of chips in a single [package](/wiki/Chip_carrier "Chip carrier"). When produced in large volumes, SoC is more cost-effective than SiP because its packaging is simpler.[[24]](#cite_note-25) Another reason SiP may be preferred is [waste heat](/wiki/Waste_heat "Waste heat") may be too high in a SoC for a given purpose because functional components are too close together, and in an SiP heat will dissipate better from different functional modules since they are physically further apart.
